roman.title: Roman Numerals
roman.summary: Additive-subtractive system used across Roman Empire.
roman.morphology: Uses symbols I, V, X, L, C, D, M with subtractive pairs like IV, IX.
roman.hierarchy: Symbols represent fixed values; order and subtraction determine totals.
roman.context: Used historically across Europe for numbering, inscriptions and clocks.

mayan.title: Mayan Numerals
mayan.summary: Vigesimal (base-20) using dots and bars; special shell for zero.
mayan.base: Base-20 positional with dot (1) and bar (5) glyphs
mayan.morphology: Glyphs stacked vertically; each level is a power of 20 (with historical exceptions).
mayan.hierarchy: Vertical place-value stacking; special treatment for the third place historically.
mayan.context: Used by Mesoamerican civilizations for calendrics and astronomy.

chinese.title: Chinese Numerals
chinese.summary: Multiplicative-additive system using characters for digits and units.
chinese.base: Decimal positional expressed with characters and unit markers
chinese.morphology: Uses characters for 0-9 and unit markers like 十,百,千.
chinese.hierarchy: Multiplicative compounds produce numbers (e.g., 二百 = 2*100).
chinese.context: Used in East Asia for millennia; forms the basis of many East Asian counting conventions.

babylonian.title: Babylonian (Base-60)
babylonian.summary: Sexagesimal positional system influential in astronomy and timekeeping.
babylonian.morphology: Composed using wedge-shaped marks; place values multiply by 60.
babylonian.hierarchy: High radix leads to compact representations for large numbers.
babylonian.context: Developed in Mesopotamia; underlies modern measures of time/angles.

yoruba.title: Yoruba Numerals
yoruba.summary: West African vigesimal system with unique morphophonology.
yoruba.morphology: Uses compounding like 'ogun' for 20; more complex expressions for many numbers.
yoruba.hierarchy: Compounds based on 20s and units; tone and morphology affect form.
yoruba.context: Used by Yoruba speakers; reflects cultural counting traditions.

inuktitut.title: Inuktitut Counting (Notes)
inuktitut.summary: Traditional body-tally methods; modern use often adopts Arabic numerals.
inuktitut.base: Traditional non-positional tallies; modern decimal usage common
inuktitut.morphology: Historic forms tied to gestures and tallying; contemporary practice uses Arabic digits.
inuktitut.hierarchy: Cultural counting tied to tasks and environment rather than abstract place-value.
inuktitut.context: Used by Inuit communities across Arctic regions.
